# RFC-0001: Large-Model SGLang Server-Arg Search Methodology

> **Version:** 0.6.0 | **Status:** normative | **Phase:** test

---

## 1. Summary

### [RFC-0001:C-SUMMARY] Summary (Informative) <a id="rfc-0001c-summary"></a>

This RFC specifies a methodology for searching the server-argument space of large
SGLang inference deployments to find configurations that best trade off throughput
against latency under a defined service-level objective (SLO).

**Scope:** The search procedure, its loop structure, the source of truth for
configurations, the optimization objective, acceptance criteria, and provenance
requirements. The methodology is model-agnostic; Nemotron-3-Ultra-550B (NVFP4/FP8)
is its first application. Speculative decoding (EAGLE / spec-decode) is explicitly
out of scope for this methodology (see [RFC-0001:C-SCOPE](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-scope)). The search is partitioned
into branches of non-substitutable deployments (see [RFC-0001:C-BRANCH](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-branch)).

**Out of scope:** The specific known-best argument values for any given model, the
results-presentation tooling beyond the requirement that a config's provenance be
recorded, and the choice of orchestration implementation.

**Rationale:** A naive full-factorial grid over server arguments × workload axes ×
precision is infeasible for 550B-class models, where every restart-required
configuration costs a full server relaunch, weight load, and warmup before a single
measurement. This RFC defines obligations that keep the search tractable, the results
comparable across runs, and the chosen configurations trustworthy.

*Since: v0.1.0*

---

## 2. Specification

### [RFC-0001:C-SCOPE] Search scope and exclusions (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-scope"></a>

The methodology MUST classify every tunable server argument into exactly one of three
buckets before a search begins: *fixed* (a known-best value that is never varied),
*candidate* (varied during the search), or *constrained* (governed by an explicit
illegal-combination rule). The candidate set SHOULD contain at most ten arguments.

Speculative decoding (EAGLE / spec-decode and equivalent draft-model techniques) MUST
NOT be treated as a candidate argument under this methodology; it is out of scope. A
search that wishes to evaluate speculative decoding MUST do so under a separate effort,
because it alters the argument taxonomy and the comparability baseline.

**Rationale:** Bounding and bucketing the argument space up front is what makes the
search tractable; an unbounded candidate set reproduces the infeasible full-factorial
grid. Speculative decoding changes both the arg taxonomy and the meaning of a
throughput number, so folding it into the same sweep would break comparability with the
non-speculative baseline.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-LOOP-STRUCTURE] Outer/inner loop separation (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-loop-structure"></a>

The search MUST be organized as two nested loops. The outer loop MUST iterate over
restart-required configuration (for example tensor/expert parallelism, attention
backend, quantization, static memory fraction, CUDA-graph capture settings, and
chunked-prefill size); each outer iteration MUST relaunch the server. The inner loop
MUST iterate over workload axes (batch size, input/output sequence lengths, and
concurrency) against a single live server without relaunching it.

Workload axes MUST NOT be expanded into the outer loop as if they were restart-required
configuration.

**Rationale:** Restart-required configuration costs a full relaunch, weight load, and
warmup per value, whereas workload axes are client-side and free to sweep against one
running server. Conflating the two multiplies expensive restarts by axes that need
none, which is the dominant cost error in a naive grid.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-BRANCH] Search branches, not grid axes (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-branch"></a>

The served model checkpoint is the outermost scope of a search and carries the weight
precision (for example NVFP4, FP8, or BF16); a different weight precision is a different
checkpoint and therefore a different model, not a branch. Within a single checkpoint, the
methodology MUST organize the search into one or more *branches*. A branch is a partition,
within that one checkpoint, whose members are not substitutable and which MUST NOT be
multiplied into the focused grid as if they were candidate arguments. A branch is identified
by its *branch keys*: the non-substitutable runtime and hardware attributes that do not
change the checkpoint. The branch keys MUST include, at minimum, the hardware target (for
example accelerator model and device count) and the KV-cache precision, and MAY include
other runtime precisions (for example a runtime-selectable activation or attention dtype).
The weight precision MUST NOT be a branch key, because it changes the checkpoint.

Each branch MUST have its own baseline, and MAY have its own fixed-argument set and candidate
set. A per-branch baseline is scoped within its branch and MUST NOT replace the cross-run
comparability anchor required by [RFC-0001:C-BASELINE-ANCHOR](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-baseline-anchor). The accuracy gate of
[RFC-0001:C-QUALITY-GATE](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-quality-gate) MUST be evaluated at least once per branch.

KV-cache precision, and any other runtime precision, MAY be modeled either as a branch key —
with its own baseline and accuracy-gate validation — or as an accuracy-active candidate
argument gated per configuration under [RFC-0001:C-QUALITY-GATE](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-quality-gate). The search MUST record,
for each such runtime precision, which treatment it chose. A branch key that is a physical attribute, such as the hardware
target, MUST also be recorded in the execution environment of [RFC-0001:C-RUN-OUTPUT](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-run-output) so
that measurements taken on different hardware are isolated rather than conflated under one
measurement identity.

**Rationale:** Branch members answer "which deployment of this checkpoint is this?" not
"which arg is best?": a different hardware target or KV-cache precision is not a drop-in
substitute, so folding them into the grid multiplies the search while comparing
configurations that cannot be exchanged. Weight precision is excluded from the branch keys
because it is not a runtime choice at all — it is baked into the weights, so it lives one
level up at the model/checkpoint, where distinct checkpoints are searched and recorded
separately. KV-cache precision is left to the author's discretion because a single extra
dtype is sometimes cheaper to gate per configuration than to stand up as a full branch.
Recording a physical branch key in the execution environment is what keeps a config tuned on
one accelerator from being served a stale measurement taken on another.

*Since: v0.6.0*

### <del>[RFC-0001:C-PRECISION-BRANCH] Precision as a branch, not a grid axis</del> (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-precision-branch"></a>

Numeric precision (for example NVFP4 versus FP8) MUST be treated as a top-level branch
of the search, not as a grid axis multiplied into every configuration. Each precision
branch MAY have its own checkpoint, fixed-argument set, and baseline. A per-branch
baseline is scoped within its branch and MUST NOT replace the cross-run comparability
anchor required by [RFC-0001:C-BASELINE-ANCHOR](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-baseline-anchor).

**Rationale:** Distinct precisions typically correspond to different checkpoints and
fundamentally different deployments; treating precision as an inner grid axis multiplies
the entire search by a factor while comparing configurations that are not
substitutable.

> **Superseded by:** RFC-0001:C-BRANCH

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-CONFIG-SOURCE] Versioned config as source of truth (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-config-source"></a>

The single source of truth for which configurations are searched MUST be a
version-controlled configuration file in the repository. A configuration generator MUST
emit only valid permutations, applying the constraint rules from [RFC-0001:C-SCOPE](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-scope) to
prune illegal combinations before any server launch.

External spreadsheet or database tools (for example Lark) MUST NOT be used as the
configuration generator. Such tools MAY be used only as a downstream results viewer.

**Rationale:** A versioned config file gives validation, incompatibility rules, and
provenance; a spreadsheet used as a generator produces invalid combinations that are
discovered only by wasting expensive launches.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-SEARCH-STRATEGY] Sensitivity-then-focused-grid search (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-search-strategy"></a>

The search MUST NOT enumerate a full-factorial grid over the candidate arguments.
Instead it MUST proceed in stages: first a one-factor-at-a-time (OFAT) sensitivity
sweep that varies a single candidate argument around a known-good baseline, then a
focused joint grid restricted to only those arguments that BOTH showed sensitivity in
the OFAT stage AND plausibly interact. Arguments that did not survive the OFAT stage
MUST be pinned to their OFAT-best value during the focused grid. The set of arguments
admitted to the focused grid, and the rationale for judging them to interact, MUST be
recorded so the selection is inspectable.

**Rationale:** Serving arguments are mostly independent, so OFAT eliminates the large
majority of the grid while revealing which arguments matter and in which direction; a
joint grid is reserved for the few arguments where interaction is plausible. Recording
the admitted set turns the otherwise-subjective interaction judgment into an inspectable
artifact.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-OBJECTIVE] Pareto frontier under an SLO (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-objective"></a>

A service-level objective (SLO) MUST be defined before the search begins. The SLO's
gating bound MUST be a decode latency bound: a per-token (inter-token) latency limit,
equivalently a minimum steady-state decode throughput. A time-to-first-token (TTFT)
target MAY also be defined, but TTFT MUST be treated as a reported, non-gating metric — a
configuration MUST NOT be excluded from the results on the basis of TTFT alone.

Decode performance is the first-class objective. The throughput axis MUST be the
steady-state decode throughput at the operating context length: the decode rate measured
over a window of consecutive decode steps at the run's longest sequence position,
excluding the first decode step after prefill. It MUST NOT be a throughput averaged over
an output sequence that mixes in inexpensive early-context tokens. The window size MUST be
fixed before the search and recorded; every decode-throughput measurement MUST be tagged
with the context length at which it was taken, so that measurements are compared only at
equivalent sequence positions.

The optimization objective MUST be the decode-throughput-versus-decode-latency Pareto
frontier under the decode SLO. Configurations MUST NOT be ranked by raw throughput alone.

The Pareto frontier MUST be constructed within a single branch ([RFC-0001:C-BRANCH](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-branch)):
only configurations belonging to the same branch MAY be placed on one comparative frontier
and ranked against each other. Configurations from different branches MUST NOT be ranked on
a shared frontier. Cross-branch performance comparison is reporting only and MUST NOT
determine a configuration's eligibility or its rank.

**Rationale:** For large-context serving, decode is memory-bandwidth-bound on the KV cache
and its cost is governed by the current sequence length, not by how that length was
reached; per-token decode latency is what a user experiences continuously, whereas TTFT is
paid once. A decode throughput averaged over a short output is dominated by warm-up
transients, and over a long output is diluted by cheap early-context tokens, so the
steady-state rate over a window at a stated context length is the only comparable signal.
Gating on TTFT would discard configurations that sustain excellent decode merely because
prefilling a long prompt is slow. A branch is a partition of non-substitutable deployments
of one checkpoint ([RFC-0001:C-BRANCH](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-branch)), so ranking a config from one branch above a config
from another would recommend a deployment the operator cannot actually substitute in — a
faster point on different hardware, or a lower-KV-precision point, would dominate one it
cannot replace — which is why the frontier is built within a branch and cross-branch
differences are reported, not ranked.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-QUALITY-GATE] Accuracy acceptance gate (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-quality-gate"></a>

A configuration MUST pass an accuracy acceptance gate before it is eligible to appear in
the acceptable results. The accuracy gate MUST be evaluated for each branch (see
[RFC-0001:C-BRANCH](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-branch)).

The gate MUST be measured strictly within a branch: a configuration passes only if its
accuracy on the evaluation dataset is no more than a fixed tolerance BELOW its OWN branch's
baseline accuracy. A configuration whose accuracy matches or exceeds its branch baseline
trivially passes; the gate bounds degradation, not deviation. The branch baseline whose
accuracy serves as this reference is the per-branch baseline of [RFC-0001:C-BRANCH](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-branch); this
maximum tolerated degradation and the evaluation dataset MUST be defined before the search
begins. A configuration's gate result MUST NOT be determined by comparison against any other
branch's baseline, tolerance, or measured accuracy. Accuracy differences between branches MAY
be reported but MUST NOT affect a configuration's gate eligibility in any branch. For this
clause, the *acceptable results* are the per-branch ranked
decode-throughput-versus-decode-latency Pareto frontier defined by [RFC-0001:C-OBJECTIVE](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-objective);
the *measurement record stream* is the full set of recorded measurements defined by
[RFC-0001:C-MEASUREMENT](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-measurement).

Each candidate argument ([RFC-0001:C-SCOPE](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-scope)) MUST be classified as *accuracy-invariant* —
its values change performance but not the model's computed outputs (for example scheduling,
memory-split, or parallelism-layout arguments) — or *accuracy-active* — its values can change
those computed outputs (for example a compute dtype, a quantization mode, or an attention/SSM
kernel backend). This classification MUST be recorded so it is inspectable, and an argument
whose effect on the computed outputs is unknown or unverified MUST be classified
accuracy-active. Fixed and constrained arguments that are accuracy-active — including the
checkpoint's weight precision and any KV-cache precision or other runtime precision treated
as a branch key ([RFC-0001:C-BRANCH](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-branch)) — are discharged by the per-branch baseline
validation defined below, not by per-configuration evaluation.

When a search varies any accuracy-active argument, the accuracy gate MUST be evaluated per
configuration as specified in the rest of this clause. When a search varies only
accuracy-invariant arguments, per-configuration evaluation MAY be skipped, provided that both
(a) the branch baseline accuracy has been measured to establish the branch reference, and
(b) a spot-check configuration that sets every varied accuracy-invariant argument to an
extreme of its range is gate-evaluated and passes — its accuracy is no more than the tolerance
below that same-branch baseline reference. The spot-check evaluation of (b) is the gate
evaluation that satisfies the at-least-once-per-branch requirement; both (a) and (b) are
required. A search that skips per-configuration evaluation MUST record that it did so together
with the spot-check configuration and its result.

Among configurations subject to per-configuration evaluation, a configuration that fails the
accuracy gate MUST be excluded from eligibility at every stage of the search, regardless of
its throughput or latency. A value whose OFAT configuration failed the gate MUST NOT be used
to pin a non-gridded argument ([RFC-0001:C-SEARCH-STRATEGY](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-search-strategy)); an accuracy-invariant
argument pinned without a per-configuration gate result is permitted, because the baseline
validation and spot-check stand in for the gate. A failing configuration MUST NOT be carried
forward as an eligible point in the focused grid. A failing configuration MUST NOT appear in
the acceptable results. If a branch has no gate-passing value with which to pin a
required non-gridded argument, or if no configuration in the branch passes the gate, the
search MUST report that branch as having no acceptable configuration rather than emitting a
degraded one.

A failing configuration's measurement MUST still be recorded in the same measurement record
stream that carries passing configurations, extended with its accuracy-gate result and a
pass/fail flag ([RFC-0001:C-MEASUREMENT](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-measurement)), so that a speed-versus-quality trade-off
remains inspectable over that stream. A failing configuration MUST NOT thereby be promoted
into the acceptable results; it is retained only for inspection and possible explicit human
override. An override that admits a flagged configuration MUST itself be recorded in the
measurement record stream.

**Rationale:** A throughput- and latency-only search will otherwise select fast but
quality-degraded configurations, which is especially likely when precision is reduced —
whether at the checkpoint level (a quantized weight precision such as NVFP4) or within a
checkpoint (a lower KV-cache precision). Fixing the threshold up front (as with the SLO in
[RFC-0001:C-OBJECTIVE](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-objective)) prevents the bar from being moved to fit the results. Measuring
the gate against the same-branch baseline rather than a cross-branch reference is what makes
the bar fair: branches within one checkpoint can have different accuracy ceilings — an
fp8-KV-cache branch sits below a higher-KV-precision one — so judging the fp8-KV-cache branch
against the higher branch's baseline would fail it for being the KV precision it was chosen to
be, not for regressing within its own branch. (Weight-precision differences are a
checkpoint-level distinction, handled above the branch.) Excluding
failures at every search stage — not only the final ranking — stops a quality-degraded value
from steering OFAT pinning or grid admission. Recording and flagging the failure in the
measurement record stream rather than discarding it preserves the measured trade-off for a
human to judge, while keeping the acceptable results free of degraded configurations. Most
serving arguments — batching, memory split, parallelism layout — are numerically neutral, so
re-evaluating accuracy for every such permutation spends eval budget without new information;
classifying arguments lets the expensive gate target only configurations that can actually
move quality. The recorded classification, the baseline validation, and an extreme-value
spot-check keep the skip auditable, and defaulting unknown arguments to accuracy-active fails
safe.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-MEASUREMENT] Warmup, repeats, and provenance (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-measurement"></a>

Each measured configuration MUST be warmed up before measurement and MUST be measured
with at least two repeat runs. Each recorded result MUST capture the full reproducibility
provenance: the configuration identity (for example a content hash of the config), the
workload point, the measured metrics, and the execution environment (at minimum the
hardware target — accelerator model and device count — the SGLang commit, the
kernel/library versions, and all cluster networking environment variables in effect). When an accuracy gate applies ([RFC-0001:C-QUALITY-GATE](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-quality-gate)), the
recorded result MUST also capture the configuration's accuracy-gate result and its
pass/fail flag.

**Rationale:** Warmup dominates first-measurement timing on 550B-class models, and a
single unrepeated measurement is noise; without recorded environment provenance, numbers
from different runs cannot be compared or reproduced. Carrying the accuracy-gate result and
pass/fail flag in the same record keeps the speed-versus-quality trade-off inspectable from
one stream ([RFC-0001:C-QUALITY-GATE](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-quality-gate)).

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-BASELINE-ANCHOR] Comparability anchor (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-baseline-anchor"></a>

The search MUST retain a stable baseline benchmark whose methodology does not change
across runs, to anchor comparability with prior results. New benchmarking tooling MAY be
introduced for richer scenario and latency-component metrics, but before it is relied
upon its results MUST be reconciled against the stable baseline benchmark on an
equivalent workload (for example confirming time-to-first-token agreement against an
already-running server).

**Rationale:** Historical baselines were produced with a specific built-in benchmark;
silently switching tools makes new numbers incomparable to the prior record. Reconciling
a new tool against the anchor before adoption prevents undetected baseline drift.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-WORKLOAD-STAGING] Workload cost-role staging (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-workload-staging"></a>

The search MUST separate workloads by cost role. Short-output workloads (a long prompt with
a small number of decode tokens, for example a 60k-token input with tens of output tokens)
MUST serve as the fast-iteration signal that drives the argument search: every decode step
of such a workload sits at approximately the same long context length, so the steady-state
decode throughput at that length (RFC-0001:C-OBJECTIVE) is obtained directly from its tail
decode window. Long-output workloads (a large number of decode tokens) MUST NOT be run
across the full candidate set, and MUST NOT be run during the OFAT or focused-grid stages
of RFC-0001:C-SEARCH-STRATEGY; they MUST be reserved for characterizing the final chosen
configuration(s) in the reported result.

A decode-throughput number obtained from a short-output fast-iteration workload at context
length L, and one obtained from a long-output workload as its decode trajectory passes
through the same context length L, are measurements of the same decode regime. When both
are available they SHOULD be reconciled within the tolerance of RFC-0001:C-BASELINE-ANCHOR;
a disagreement beyond that tolerance MUST be treated as an unreconciled measurement.

**Rationale:** Running a long-output workload across the full candidate set multiplies the
most expensive part of the search — sustained decode — across every configuration, which is
the cost error this methodology exists to avoid. Because decode throughput depends only on
the current context length, a cheap short-output run at a long prompt length yields the same
steady-state decode signal needed to rank arguments, leaving the expensive long-output
characterization for the few configurations that survive the search."""

*Since: v0.2.0*

### [RFC-0001:C-RUN-OUTPUT] Run-scoped, content-addressed result output (Normative) <a id="rfc-0001c-run-output"></a>

A single measurement MUST be identified by the content of its measured inputs: the
configuration identity ([RFC-0001:C-MEASUREMENT](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-measurement)); the workload point; the benchmark tool
used ([RFC-0001:C-BASELINE-ANCHOR](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-baseline-anchor)); and the execution environment recorded under
[RFC-0001:C-MEASUREMENT](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-measurement) — the hardware target (accelerator model and device count),
which is a branch key of [RFC-0001:C-BRANCH](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-branch); the SGLang commit; the kernel/library
versions; and the cluster networking environment. A timestamp MUST NOT be part of this identity; it MAY be recorded as
metadata. The recorded results of a search for one benchmark tool and one execution environment
are its *result set*.

A search SHOULD reuse an existing measurement whose identity matches, rather than re-measure it,
unless re-measurement is explicitly requested; this makes extending a search incremental —
adding workload points or candidate configurations measures only the identities not already
recorded, leaving prior measurements untouched. An explicit re-measurement that overrides a
matching identity MUST be recorded. Because the execution environment is part of the identity, a
change to it — a new SGLang/kernel build, library versions, or networking environment — has no
matching prior measurement and MUST be re-measured rather than reused, isolating results by
execution environment.

Recorded results MUST be isolated so that a measurement is never silently overwritten by, or
conflated with, a measurement of a different identity, and result sets for different execution
environments MUST NOT be merged. Each result set MUST carry a manifest recording the search that
produced it and the execution environment, alongside the per-result reproducibility provenance
of [RFC-0001:C-MEASUREMENT](../rfc/RFC-0001.md#rfc-0001c-measurement); the manifest MUST be written even when the search yields no
eligible result. The on-disk layout (directory and file naming) is not fixed by this clause;
only the measurement-identity, reuse-and-re-measure, isolation, and manifest obligations are
normative.

**Rationale:** A search is extended incrementally — more concurrencies, more candidates — so
keying reuse on the whole invocation would force redoing unchanged measurements; keying it on
each measurement's own inputs makes extensions and re-runs cheap and reproducible. Folding the
full execution environment (build, libraries, and networking) into the identity stops a change
that alters the numbers — a kernel upgrade or an NCCL/transport env change — from being served a
stale, non-comparable measurement; excluding the timestamp ensures the same measured point
always resolves to the same identity. Folding the hardware target into the environment is
what keeps a config tuned on one accelerator from being served a measurement taken on
another, since the optimal configuration differs by hardware. The build-scoped isolation this yields is what
[RFC-0002:C-RANK-VALIDITY](../rfc/RFC-0002.md#rfc-0002c-rank-validity) relies on when it scopes rankings to a revision.

*Since: v0.5.0*

---

## Changelog

### v0.6.0 (2026-06-10)

Generalize the precision branch into a within-checkpoint branch; scope accuracy gate and Pareto frontier within a branch

#### Added

- C-BRANCH — a branch is a partition WITHIN one checkpoint, keyed by hardware target + KV-cache precision (+ optional other runtime precision); weight precision defines the checkpoint/model, not a branch key

#### Changed

- C-PRECISION-BRANCH superseded by C-BRANCH
- C-QUALITY-GATE — accuracy gate is within-branch and baseline-relative (one-sided: no more than a fixed tolerance below the config's own branch baseline); cross-branch comparison forbidden for eligibility
- C-OBJECTIVE — Pareto frontier constructed within a single branch; cross-branch comparison is reporting only
- C-MEASUREMENT / C-RUN-OUTPUT — execution environment includes the hardware target (accelerator model + device count)

### v0.5.0 (2026-06-10)

Add C-RUN-OUTPUT: content-addressed measurement identity + reuse (incremental sweeps), execution-environment isolation, and a per-result-set manifest

### v0.4.0 (2026-06-09)

C-QUALITY-GATE: accuracy-invariant/active candidate classification — searches varying only accuracy-invariant args MAY skip per-config gating (baseline + extreme-value spot-check); C-MEASUREMENT records the accuracy-gate result + pass/fail flag

### v0.3.0 (2026-06-09)

Tighten C-QUALITY-GATE: accuracy gate filters eligibility at every search stage (OFAT pinning, focused-grid admission, final results); failing configs recorded and flagged in the measurement stream for trade-off inspection, never promoted into the acceptable results

### v0.2.0 (2026-06-09)

Decode-first objective; TTFT becomes non-gating; workload cost-role staging

#### Added

- C-WORKLOAD-STAGING — short-output workloads drive the search; long-output workloads reserved for final-report characterization

#### Changed

- C-OBJECTIVE — decode latency/throughput is the first-class objective and the sole SLO gate; TTFT is now a reported, non-gating metric
- C-OBJECTIVE — throughput axis is steady-state decode throughput tagged with context length, not OSL-averaged

### v0.1.0 (2026-06-09)

Initial draft
